reasoning: The Federal Reserve's 4-week Treasury bill rate has been very stable recently, fluctuating between 3.63 and 3.65, with the most recent value (March 13) at 3.64. The target comparison is between March 15 (value: 3.64) and March 22. Crucially, the FOMC meeting concludes on March 18, releasing an updated dot plot and policy statement. CME FedWatch data shows a 96% probability of a rate hold at this meeting, so the actual policy rate will almost certainly remain unchanged. However, the short-term 4-week bill yield is highly sensitive to the Fed's dot plot and future rate expectations. Recent search results indicate rising inflation expectations, an "oil shock" from an Iran conflict, and stagflation fears. In January, two FOMC members even wanted a rate hike. This macro backdrop strongly suggests the March 18 dot plot will tilt hawkishly (potentially signaling "higher for longer" or fewer cuts in 2026 than markets originally hoped). Even a slight hawkish tilt without an actual rate hike will likely push the 4-week T-bill yield up by a base point or two (e.g., from 3.64 to 3.65). The FRED statistical analysis estimates a 65% chance of the value exceeding 3.64. Combining the statistical anchor with the hawkish fundamental outlook, I estimate a 67% probability of the rate increasing by March 22.
